Now, if the market assigns 8x FCF, we have ~$60/share, while if it assigns 10x FCF, we get ~$75/share. This range does not require higher oil prices, production growth – nothing. It only takes in management guidance on synergies and buybacks.

The market is currently discounting the durability of the ~$7.50 per share number. Now, this number derives from synergies and buybacks. The former are operational and within management’s control.

The buybacks come from capital availability. Now, assuming post-merger FCF is $3.9B, and dividend payment comes to around $800M, the company is left with ~$3B in FCF to fund the $5B buyback. Therefore, within a two-year window, this is entirely possible.

It appears that at ~6.4 Fwd FCF multiples, the market is pricing in unreasonable doubt.

A Higher Floor

The idea that Devon now has a higher floor is visible in the numbers. As we have seen, there is now a ~50% increase in per-share earnings power without any corresponding change in oil prices. The key implication is not the upside alone, but the fact that Devon now has less downside-sensitivity.

We can see that using a conservative 6-7 FCF framework and the pre- and post-merger per share FCF values:

We can see that DVN now sits right within the latter range – a range which has been derived without any relation to oil prices. That implies that even if oil prices normalize, DVN share gets support at the $45 level - limiting its downside. Upside remains tied to oil prices, but the downside is now largely decoupled from it.

How about upside, then? At current elevated oil prices, we have seen EV/FCF at around 12x. Even if oil normalizes a little, but still stays stronger than pre-crisis baseline, we can assume 8x or even 10x FCF. Now,

8x FCF → ~$60/share

10x FCF → ~$75/share

That is ~25–55% upside, driven by re-rating, not oil.

What Investors Actually Get

The benefit of this is best seen in where cash flow gets allocated.

At ~$7.50 per share in free cash flow, and a post-merger dividend of approximately ~$1.26 per share, the payout ratio is:

1.26 / 7.50 ≈ ~17%

This is much lower than the current ~24–25% range, because it counts in synergies and buybacks and therefore reflects a more conservative and flexible capital return structure.

That retained ~$6.2+ per share is what funds the buyback. The impact is now straightforward:

  • ~16–17% reduction in share count

  • ~15–20% uplift in per-share metrics

  • higher ownership per investor

The shift from external dependence to internal control is what defines Devon’s higher floor.

Conclusion

Devon’s share price going up recently may have been caused by the war premium, however, my argument is that the Coterra merger gives it a floor beyond which — once having gone up — it will not fall. The merger will fundamentally change the business, producing synergies that didn’t exist earlier. That, plus the buyback, will give it the floor it needs to stay where it is, at the least.

If the market assigns a slightly higher multiple, the stock can have ~25–55% upside from current levels.

Thus, the downside is supported, so this is not a Sell, however, the upside remains tied to oil and potential re-rating, so this is not a hard Buy either. Given that, DVN stock is fairly valued today, and I would rate it a Hold with an upside bias.

data.title: CNH Industrial: Weak Fundamentals And Elevated Valuation Support Continued Downside
data.description:
Following our recent deep dive into Exor N.V. (EXXRF), we are back to analyzing one of its main holdings, CNH Industrial (NYSE:CNH). For our new readers, Exor is the holding company that owns 26.9% of CNH Industrial's economic rights, and we believe it trades at an NAV discount of more than 60%. Going back to CNH Industrial on a standalone basis, in our analysis, we downgraded the company with a sell in June 2025, and this proved to be the right call (Fig. 1). As a reminder, our sell rating was supported by 1) lower guidance, 2) margin pressure combined with limited visibility on earnings and 3) higher inventory levels. With a demanding valuation, our assessment was relatively straightforward.

Fig 1

Why are we still negative?

On a YTD basis, the company's share price increased by almost 25%. Q4 top-line sales increased by 6%, but on a full-year basis, CNH turnover declined by 9%. At the net income level, Q4 profit reached $89 million, with full-year profit of $505 million. This represents an EPS of $0.41. At the current price, the company trades at a P/E of almost 30x. Aside from the valuation, Q4 EPS of $0.19 beat the consensus of $0.10; however, the EPS beat was mainly driven by a pull-forward of Agriculture machinery sales from 2026 to Q4 2025. This was due to Eastern European dealers' higher inventory. Therefore, we are not surprised to see a lower 2026 outlook for the segment. Looking at the press release, the company directly reported that "amid persistent agricultural equipment market challenges, the Company is preparing for lower demand levels in 2026 ahead of the start of an expected industry recovery in 2027." While some may argue that tensions in the Middle East could support agricultural profitability by tightening pesticide supply, we do not expect this to translate into a positive year for the sector. Here at the Lab, we notice a short-term price rebound in soybeans, corn, etc., but we believe there is no change in fundamentals. Therefore, we believe the CNH Agriculture business is still in a wait-and-see phase.

Fig 2

Here at the Lab, we also report an additional downside risk not previously mentioned. Through UBS, we participated in an expert call with a former senior executive at a large EPC contractor. He highlighted the growing severity of construction labor shortages driven by AI CAPEX. According to his view, current constraints are more acute than in previous cycles, driven by strong demand from large-scale projects, particularly data centers, and by reduced labor availability. This tightening in the labor market is likely to pressure margins across CNH Industrial’s customer base and may help explain the weaker margin trajectory observed in the segment. For 2026, the company expects adjusted core operating profit to be between 1% and 2% (Fig. 3). One could argue that the CNH Agriculture business is cyclical. Still, we believe the Construction division could suffer more from AI-related CAPEX competition.

Fig 3

Earnings Change and Valuation

Despite the Q4 beat in the Agriculture segment, the company's guidance reflects ongoing sluggish trends. Even if we might anticipate better results in H2 on geographic mix, lower SG&A, and better inventory management, this is a great story to underwrite. Overall, we have limited visibility into sales for the year, and we do not expect a recovery in 2027. Our initial assessment was to revisit the company with a fresh perspective as our interest in the agricultural segment strengthens; however, we received negative evidence. Additionally, the company will increase its CAPEX, which will reduce its FCF. On an FCF yield, CNH trades at 1.7%. FCF evolution was already a big disappointment in 2024 (it was negative for $400 million). While Q4 was better than feared, part of this result was due to sales being moved from 2026 to Q4 2025. Looking back, we assumed a net profit of $726 million for 2025, but the company delivered a net income of $505 million. With lower sales and a decremental EBIT margin (Fig. 4), we see more downside risks than upside. Even assuming the company’s EPS of $0.4, CNH trades at a 29x P/E in 2026, and our expectations for 2027 are not rosy. This is related to continuous pressure in the CNH construction division and no change in demand in the agriculture segment.

Additionally, we believe the company may trim its dividend. With the current payout, CNH usually pays more than $300 million to shareholders; however, given the CAPEX investment, CNH may need to increase its leverage to finance shareholder remuneration. This is also detrimental to investors. Again, our team usually does not change our valuation methodology. For CNH, this was based on its 5-year historical average (Fig. 5). Last time, we made an exception, applying a 15x P/E recovery multiple (and, continuing to do that, our price target is set at $6 per share, vs. a current market price of $11.7). At this stage, even applying the sector median peers with a 20x P/E, we think CNH is still a sell.

Fig 4

Fig 5

Risks

We have long covered CNH downside risks, and we report additional headwinds in our analysis. The company is exposed to interest rate risk (both on its balance sheet and in its client base). CNH interest rates might also impact its residual value risk in the financial services segment. The company is exposed to changes in commodity prices and lower global consumption. This is related to market cycles in the Agricultural Equipment and Construction segments. CNH risks also include FX and failure in product innovation. As reported in the analysis, CNH might cut its dividend per share due to FCF evolution. This might result in multiple compression.

Conclusion

Higher inflation expectations and a market softening will drag the company’s valuation down in our view. We are surprised by the share price evolution, but we need to report on already low investor expectations. Given the declining segments and limited visibility for 2027, we continue to recommend a sell.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
